text: One session per week of interval training at vVO2max (5 repetitions at 50% time limit with equal recovery at 60% vVO2max) for 4 weeks significantly improves vVO2max from 20.5 to 21.1 km/h.
Improvement in vVO2max from interval training can occur through improved running economy rather than increased VO2max itself.
specific_value: running economy improved from 50.6 to 47.5 mL/min/kg, P=0.02

text: Tripling interval training volume at vVO2max (from 1 to 3 sessions per week) for 4 weeks does not produce additional improvements in aerobic performance or vVO2max beyond what one session per week achieves.
Submaximal heart rate at a fixed pace decreases with both normal and overload interval training at vVO2max, indicating cardiovascular adaptation even when performance metrics plateau.
specific_value: 162 to 155 bpm after NT, 155 to 150 bpm after OT at 14 km/h

text: Increasing vVO2max interval training from 1 to 3 sessions per week causes a significant rise in post-exercise plasma norepinephrine, a potential overtraining marker, without performance gains.
Optimal interval training at vVO2max uses repetitions of 50% of individual time-to-exhaustion at vVO2max with equal-duration recovery at 60% vVO2max to maximize time spent at VO2max.
specific_value: 50% Tlim work, equal recovery at 60% vVO2max

text: Lactate threshold as a percentage of vVO2max remains unchanged after 4 weeks of once-weekly vVO2max interval training.
